test.name: Historical legal terminology in an 1832 will
test.description: Tests whether the skill correctly explains archaic legal terms commonly found in 18th and 19th century English and American wills — specifically 'relict' (surviving spouse) and 'appurtenances' (attached property/rights). Tests terminology lookup and research-actionable guidance.

input.user_message: An 1832 will for my ancestor uses the words 'relict' and 'appurtenances.' What do these mean and how do they help my research?

judge_context: Must correctly define 'relict' as the surviving spouse — most commonly a widow, though it can also refer to a widower — confirming the spouse was still living when the will or probate proceeding was created
Must correctly define 'appurtenances' as rights, privileges, land, buildings, easements, or other property interests attached to and passing with the principal property
Must explain that identifying the relict in a will is a key genealogical finding — it names the surviving spouse and confirms the marital relationship
Should note that legal terminology can vary slightly by jurisdiction and time period and should be interpreted in the context of the whole document
Should direct researchers to specific record types: probate files, wills and codicils, estate inventories, land deeds, court records, and guardianship records
Should note that references to appurtenances may reveal the full extent of an estate and associated rights not obvious from a simple property description
